name: Corporate Finance
description: A rigorous introduction to corporate finance covering financial statement analysis, time value of money, risk and return, capital budgeting, capital structure, dividend policy, and international finance with an emphasis on ethical decision-making.

modules.title: Introduction to Corporate Finance
modules.objectives: Define corporate finance and the three core decisions (investment, financing, dividend); explain the goal of financial management (shareholder wealth maximization); understand agency relationships and conflicts.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 1 — The Corporation
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Corporate Finance
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Corporate Finance Introduction Practice
modules.assignment: Identify the three fundamental finance decisions for a company of your choice (Apple, Tesla, or similar); research and explain one real investment decision, one financing decision, and one dividend decision the company made in the past two years.

modules.title: Financial Statements & Analysis
modules.objectives: Read and interpret the income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement; calculate and interpret key financial ratios (liquidity, profitability, leverage, efficiency); understand DuPont analysis.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 5 — Financial Statements
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Analyze Financial Statements
modules.assignment: Download a public company's latest annual report (10-K) from SEC.gov; calculate five financial ratios from the income statement and balance sheet; compare them to the industry average and write a one-page analysis of the company's financial health.

modules.title: Time Value of Money
modules.objectives: Explain why money has a time value; calculate present value and future value for single amounts and annuities; solve for any variable (PV, FV, rate, nper) in TVM equations; apply TVM to real-world financial decisions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 7 — Time Value of Money
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Time Value of Money
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Present Value and Future Value Explained
modules.assignment: Solve five TVM problems: (1) FV of $5,000 in 10 years at 6%, (2) PV of $10,000 due in 5 years at 8%, (3) FV of $200/month annuity for 20 years at 5%, (4) monthly payment on a $20,000 car loan at 4% for 4 years, (5) rate needed to double $1,000 in 7 years.

modules.title: Risk & Return
modules.objectives: Calculate expected return and standard deviation; explain systematic vs. unsystematic risk; apply the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) to estimate required returns; understand portfolio diversification and beta.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 15 — Risk and Return
modules.video2Note: YouTube — CAPM: Capital Asset Pricing Model Explained
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Risk and Return Practice
modules.assignment: Given three stocks with different expected returns and standard deviations, calculate their expected returns using CAPM (assume risk-free rate = 3%, market return = 10%); explain how combining them in a portfolio reduces unsystematic risk.

modules.title: Capital Budgeting: NPV & IRR
modules.titleZh: 资本预算：净现值与内部收益率
modules.objectives: Apply NPV and IRR methods to evaluate capital investment projects; understand the relationship between NPV and firm value; resolve conflicts between NPV and IRR rankings; apply the profitability index for capital rationing.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 12 — Capital Budgeting
modules.video2Note: YouTube — NPV vs IRR: Which Method Is Better?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — NPV and IRR Practice
modules.assignment: A company is considering two mutually exclusive projects: Project A costs $100,000 with cash flows of $35,000/year for 4 years; Project B costs $100,000 with cash flows of $20,000, $30,000, $40,000, $60,000 over 4 years. Using a 10% discount rate, calculate NPV and IRR for each and recommend which to choose.

modules.title: Cost of Capital
modules.objectives: Calculate the cost of debt, preferred stock, and common equity; compute the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC); understand how WACC is used as a discount rate; explain the impact of capital structure on WACC.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 16 — The Cost of Capital
modules.video2Note: YouTube — WACC Explained Step by Step
modules.assignment: A company has $4M in equity (cost = 12%), $2M in preferred stock (cost = 8%), and $4M in debt (pre-tax cost = 6%, tax rate = 25%). Calculate the WACC; explain what it means for project selection decisions.

modules.title: Capital Structure & Leverage
modules.objectives: Explain Modigliani-Miller propositions in perfect and imperfect markets; analyze the trade-off between debt tax shields and financial distress costs; calculate the degree of operating and financial leverage.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 17 — Capital Structure
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Capital Structure Theory: Modigliani-Miller Explained
modules.assignment: A firm can finance a $10M project with 100% equity or 50% debt/50% equity. Assuming a 25% tax rate and 7% interest on debt, show how the interest tax shield affects firm value; calculate the degree of financial leverage at two different EBIT levels.

modules.title: Dividend Policy
modules.objectives: Explain the dividend irrelevance theory (M&M); analyze signaling and clientele effects of dividend changes; compare cash dividends, stock dividends, and stock buybacks; evaluate payout policy decisions in practice.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 18 — Dividend Policy
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Dividends and Dividend Policy
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Dividend Policy: Cash Dividends vs Stock Buybacks
modules.assignment: A company has $5 per share in earnings and is deciding between paying a $2 dividend or buying back stock at the current market price of $40. Analyze the shareholder wealth implications of each option using M&M dividend irrelevance; discuss signaling effects.

modules.title: Working Capital Management
modules.objectives: Calculate net working capital and the cash conversion cycle; analyze cash, inventory, and accounts receivable management strategies; evaluate short-term versus long-term financing for working capital.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 19 — Working Capital Management
modules.assignment: A retailer has inventory of $500,000 (sold in 45 days), accounts receivable of $300,000 (collected in 30 days), and accounts payable of $200,000 (paid in 20 days). Calculate the cash conversion cycle and suggest two strategies to improve it.

modules.title: Short-Term Financing
modules.objectives: Compare short-term financing sources: trade credit, bank lines of credit, commercial paper, and factoring; calculate the effective cost of trade credit; understand inventory financing and accounts receivable loans.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 19.3 — Short-Term Financing
modules.assignment: A supplier offers terms of '2/10 net 30' (2% discount if paid in 10 days, full amount due in 30 days). Calculate the annualized cost of not taking the discount; determine whether the firm should borrow at 8% annual to take advantage of the discount.

modules.title: Long-Term Financing & Bonds
modules.objectives: Value bonds using the present value of future cash flows; understand bond terminology (coupon, par, YTM, duration); explain the inverse relationship between bond price and interest rates; compare straight debt, convertible bonds, and zero-coupon bonds.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Financial Management Ch 10 — Bonds and Bond Valuation
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bond Valuation: Pricing and Yield to Maturity
modules.assignment: A $1,000 par bond pays a 6% annual coupon for 10 years. Calculate the bond's price if the market yield is (a) 6%, (b) 8%, and (c) 4%. Graph the bond price-yield relationship and explain the inverse relationship.

quizQuestions.question: The primary goal of financial management in a corporation is to:
quizQuestions.answer: Maximize shareholder wealth (stock price)
quizQuestions.explanation: The standard finance objective is to maximize the wealth of shareholders, measured by stock price — which reflects the present value of all expected future cash flows to the firm.

quizQuestions.question: The agency problem in corporate finance refers to:
options: Conflicts of interest between managers and shareholders
Legal disputes between the firm and its creditors
quizQuestions.answer: Conflicts of interest between managers and shareholders
quizQuestions.explanation: The principal-agent problem arises because hired managers (agents) may pursue their own interests (salary, perks, empire-building) rather than maximizing shareholder (principal) wealth.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The primary goal of financial management in a corporation is to:" is: Maximize shareholder wealth (stock price).
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Maximize shareholder wealth (stock price) is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Fill in the blank: Complete: In the context of the topic, ___ refers to "stakeholders in a corporation include all except:."
quizQuestions.answer: Competitors
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Competitors.

quizQuestions.question: Which financial statement shows a company's revenues, expenses, and profit over a period?
quizQuestions.answer: Income statement
quizQuestions.explanation: The income statement (P&L) reports revenues, costs, and expenses over an accounting period, resulting in net income — a measure of profitability.

quizQuestions.question: The current ratio measures a company's:
options: Ability to pay short-term obligations with current assets
quizQuestions.answer: Ability to pay short-term obligations with current assets
quizQuestions.explanation: Current ratio = Current assets / Current liabilities. A ratio above 1.0 indicates the company can cover its short-term debts with its short-term assets.

quizQuestions.question: If a company has net income of $200,000 and revenues of $2,000,000, its net profit margin is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Net profit margin = Net income / Revenue = $200,000 / $2,000,000 = 10%. It measures what percentage of each sales dollar converts to profit.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which financial statement shows a company's revenues, expenses, and profit over a period" is: Income statement.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Income statement is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The time value of money principle states that:
options: Money is worth more in the future than today
A dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future
Interest rates have no effect on money's value over time
Inflation always decreases the value of savings
quizQuestions.answer: A dollar today is worth more than a dollar in the future
quizQuestions.explanation: A dollar today can be invested to earn a return, making it worth more than a dollar promised in the future. This is the foundation of all present and future value calculations.

quizQuestions.question: If you invest $1,000 today at 5% annual interest, what will it be worth in 3 years?
The present value of $10,000 to be received in 4 years at a discount rate of 6% is approximately:
Beta in the CAPM framework measures a stock's:
options: Systematic risk relative to the overall market
quizQuestions.answer: Systematic risk relative to the overall market
quizQuestions.explanation: Beta measures a stock's volatility relative to the market: beta > 1 means more volatile than the market; beta < 1 means less volatile; beta = 1 means moves with the market.

quizQuestions.question: Unsystematic risk can be eliminated through:
options: Portfolio diversification across many different assets
quizQuestions.answer: Portfolio diversification across many different assets
quizQuestions.explanation: Unsystematic (company-specific) risk is diversifiable — by holding many uncorrelated assets, individual company risks offset each other, leaving only market-wide systematic risk.

quizQuestions.question: According to CAPM, the required return on a stock equals the risk-free rate plus:
quizQuestions.answer: Beta × (Market return − Risk-free rate)
quizQuestions.explanation: CAPM: Required return = Rf + β × (Rm − Rf). The equity risk premium (Rm − Rf) is scaled by beta to reflect the stock's systematic risk relative to the market.

quizQuestions.question: If the risk-free rate is 3%, market return is 10%, and a stock has a beta of 1.5, its required return is:
A project should be accepted according to NPV analysis if:
options: NPV is negative but close to zero
IRR is less than the discount rate
quizQuestions.answer: NPV is greater than zero
quizQuestions.explanation: A positive NPV means the project creates value — its present value of future cash flows exceeds its cost. Accepting positive NPV projects increases the firm's (and shareholders') value.

quizQuestions.question: The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate that makes a project's NPV equal to:
quizQuestions.answer: Zero
quizQuestions.explanation: IRR is the discount rate at which NPV = 0 — it represents the expected rate of return on the project. Accept the project if IRR > cost of capital (WACC).

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A project should be accepted according to NPV analysis if:" is: NPV is greater than zero.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. NPV is greater than zero is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate that makes a project's NPV equal to:" is: The initial investment.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Zero.

quizQuestions.question: WACC is used in capital budgeting as the:
options: Minimum required rate of return (hurdle rate) for new projects
quizQuestions.answer: Minimum required rate of return (hurdle rate) for new projects
quizQuestions.explanation: WACC represents the average cost of the firm's capital — new projects must earn at least WACC to create value. Projects earning less than WACC destroy shareholder value.

quizQuestions.question: The cost of debt in WACC calculations is adjusted for taxes because:
options: Interest payments are tax-deductible, reducing the effective cost of debt
Debt holders have lower required returns
The government sets the after-tax cost of debt
quizQuestions.answer: Interest payments are tax-deductible, reducing the effective cost of debt
quizQuestions.explanation: After-tax cost of debt = Pre-tax rate × (1 − Tax rate). The government effectively subsidizes debt financing by allowing interest deductions, making debt cheaper than its stated rate.

quizQuestions.question: A firm's WACC will increase when it:
options: Replaces expensive equity with cheaper debt
Issues more common stock and reduces its debt level
quizQuestions.answer: Issues more common stock and reduces its debt level
quizQuestions.explanation: Equity is generally more expensive than debt (higher required return). Shifting from cheaper debt to more expensive equity raises the weighted average cost of capital.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "WACC is used in capital budgeting as the:" is: Minimum required rate of return (hurdle rate) for new projects.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Minimum required rate of return (hurdle rate) for new projects is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The Modigliani-Miller theorem in a world without taxes states that:
options: Firms should use maximum debt to minimize WACC
A firm's value is unaffected by its capital structure
Equity is always preferable to debt
quizQuestions.answer: A firm's value is unaffected by its capital structure
quizQuestions.explanation: In a perfect market without taxes or frictions, M&M showed that capital structure is irrelevant — value depends on asset returns, not how they are financed.

quizQuestions.question: The interest tax shield from debt arises because:
options: Interest payments are tax-deductible, creating a government subsidy for debt financing
quizQuestions.answer: Interest payments are tax-deductible, creating a government subsidy for debt financing
quizQuestions.explanation: The tax deductibility of interest reduces a firm's tax bill — the present value of these tax savings (interest tax shield) adds to firm value, making some debt beneficial.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The Modigliani-Miller theorem in a world without taxes states that:" is: A firm's value is unaffected by its capital structure.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A firm's value is unaffected by its capital structure is correct.

quizQuestions.question: According to the dividend irrelevance theory (M&M), firm value is NOT affected by:
options: Investment decisions and operating cash flows
Whether dividends are paid or earnings are retained
quizQuestions.answer: Whether dividends are paid or earnings are retained
quizQuestions.explanation: M&M's dividend irrelevance theorem states that in perfect markets, investors can create their own 'homemade dividends' by selling shares — so dividend policy does not affect firm value.

quizQuestions.question: Dividend signaling theory suggests that when a company cuts its dividend, investors typically:
options: View it positively as the company is retaining capital
View it negatively as a signal of weaker future earnings
Have no reaction since dividends are irrelevant
quizQuestions.answer: View it negatively as a signal of weaker future earnings
quizQuestions.explanation: Managers who know more than investors use dividend changes as credible signals. A dividend cut signals that management expects lower future cash flows — typically causing the stock price to drop.

quizQuestions.question: A stock buyback (repurchase) is often preferred over a dividend because:
options: It always increases the stock price immediately
It is taxed at lower capital gains rates and is more flexible than a committed dividend
It eliminates the need to issue new shares
It always signals stronger growth than dividends
quizQuestions.answer: It is taxed at lower capital gains rates and is more flexible than a committed dividend
quizQuestions.explanation: Share repurchases allow shareholders to choose when (and whether) to realize gains (taxed at capital gains rates), and companies can adjust buyback levels more easily than dividends without negative signaling.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "According to the dividend irrelevance theory (M&M), firm value is NOT affected by:" is: Whether dividends are paid or earnings are retained.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Whether dividends are paid or earnings are retained is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Net working capital is calculated as:
quizQuestions.answer: Current assets minus current liabilities
quizQuestions.explanation: NWC = Current assets − Current liabilities. It measures a firm's short-term liquidity and operational buffer — the capital tied up in day-to-day operations.

quizQuestions.question: The cash conversion cycle (CCC) measures the time between:
options: When a company is founded and when it first earns profit
Cash paid for inventory and cash received from customers
When dividends are declared and when they are paid
Issuing bonds and making the first interest payment
quizQuestions.answer: Cash paid for inventory and cash received from customers
quizQuestions.explanation: CCC = Days inventory outstanding + Days sales outstanding − Days payable outstanding. A shorter CCC means faster cash recovery — a sign of efficient working capital management.

quizQuestions.question: A company can shorten its cash conversion cycle by:
options: Collecting receivables faster and/or paying payables later
quizQuestions.answer: Collecting receivables faster and/or paying payables later
quizQuestions.explanation: Faster collections reduce Days Sales Outstanding; taking longer to pay suppliers increases Days Payable Outstanding — both reduce CCC and improve cash flow.

quizQuestions.question: Trade credit (accounts payable) is an attractive source of short-term financing because:
options: It carries the highest interest rate
It is automatically available from suppliers with no formal application and may be interest-free within terms
It is only available to large corporations
quizQuestions.answer: It is automatically available from suppliers with no formal application and may be interest-free within terms
quizQuestions.explanation: Trade credit is the most widely used short-term financing source — suppliers extend it automatically as part of the business relationship, often with no interest if paid within terms (e.g., net 30).

quizQuestions.question: The annual cost of NOT taking a 2/10 net 30 trade discount is approximately:
quizQuestions.explanation: Cost = (Discount% / (1 − Discount%)) × (365 / (Net days − Discount period)) = (0.02 / 0.98) × (365 / 20) ≈ 2.04% × 18.25 ≈ 37.2%, approximately 36.5%.

quizQuestions.question: A bank line of credit for a business is best described as:
options: A long-term loan requiring monthly principal payments
A pre-approved borrowing limit the business can draw on as needed and repay flexibly
A permanent equity investment from a bank
A bond issued to retail investors
quizQuestions.answer: A pre-approved borrowing limit the business can draw on as needed and repay flexibly
quizQuestions.explanation: A line of credit (revolver) lets businesses borrow up to a preset limit, repay, and reborrow — ideal for seasonal inventory needs or covering timing mismatches in cash flows.

quizQuestions.question: Factoring accounts receivable means a company:
options: Waits patiently for customers to pay their invoices
Sells its receivables to a third party at a discount for immediate cash
quizQuestions.answer: Sells its receivables to a third party at a discount for immediate cash
quizQuestions.explanation: Factoring converts accounts receivable to immediate cash by selling them to a factor (financial company) at a discount — accelerating cash inflows at the cost of the discount.

quizQuestions.question: The price of a bond is equal to the present value of:
options: Only the face value at maturity
All future coupon payments plus the par value at maturity
The coupon rate times the par value
The yield to maturity minus the coupon rate
quizQuestions.answer: All future coupon payments plus the par value at maturity
quizQuestions.explanation: A bond's price = PV of all coupon payments (annuity) + PV of par value at maturity (lump sum), both discounted at the market yield (YTM).

quizQuestions.question: When market interest rates rise above a bond's coupon rate, the bond will trade:
options: At its face value plus accrued interest
quizQuestions.answer: Below par (at a discount)
quizQuestions.explanation: When market yields exceed the coupon rate, the bond's fixed payments are below what new bonds offer — investors only buy it at a lower (discounted) price to achieve the market yield.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The price of a bond is equal to the present value of:" is: All future coupon payments plus the par value at maturity.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. All future coupon payments plus the par value at maturity is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "When market interest rates rise above a bond's coupon rate, the bond will trade:" is: At par value.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Below par (at a discount).

questions.question: Which of the three core corporate finance decisions relates to how the firm raises the capital needed for investments?
questions.answer: Financing decision
questions.explanation: The financing decision determines the mix of debt and equity used to fund the firm's assets — including bond issuance, equity offerings, bank loans, and other capital-raising activities.

questions.question: A company has current assets of $500,000 and current liabilities of $200,000. Its current ratio is:
questions.explanation: Current ratio = Current assets / Current liabilities = $500,000 / $200,000 = 2.5. A ratio of 2.5 means the company has $2.50 in current assets for every $1 of current liabilities.

questions.question: What is the future value of $2,000 invested for 5 years at an annual rate of 7%?
True or False: The answer to "Using CAPM with Rf = 2%, Rm = 9%, and β = 1.2, the required return is:" is: 10.4%.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. 10.4% is correct.

questions.question: A project costs $50,000 and generates cash flows of $15,000/year for 5 years. At a 10% discount rate, the NPV is approximately:
A firm has $6M equity (cost 14%), $4M debt (pre-tax cost 6%, tax rate 30%). WACC =
The Modigliani-Miller theorem WITH corporate taxes concludes that firms should:
options: Use zero debt to maximize equity value
Use as much debt as possible to maximize the interest tax shield
Issue preferred stock instead of debt
questions.answer: Use as much debt as possible to maximize the interest tax shield
questions.explanation: With corporate taxes and no other imperfections, M&M Proposition I with taxes shows that firm value increases linearly with debt due to the tax shield — implying 100% debt is optimal (before considering distress costs).

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "In the Gordon Growth Model, if D₁ = $3, r = 12%, and g = 4%, the stock's intrinsic value is:" is: $25.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: $37.50.

questions.question: A $1,000 bond with a 5% coupon rate has a market yield of 7%. The bond will trade:
questions.answer: Below par (discount)
questions.explanation: When the market yield (7%) exceeds the coupon rate (5%), the bond's fixed payments are less than what new bonds offer at market rates — so it trades at a discount below par.

questions.question: Which financial ratio measures how efficiently a company converts sales into profit?
questions.answer: Net profit margin
questions.explanation: Net profit margin = Net income / Revenue. It measures the percentage of each sales dollar that converts to profit after all expenses.

questions.question: An annuity of $500 per year for 10 years at 6% discount rate has an approximate present value of:
True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is an example of unsystematic (diversifiable) risk" is: A CEO fraud scandal specific to one company.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. A CEO fraud scandal specific to one company is correct.

questions.question: The net working capital of a firm is best described as:
options: Total assets minus total long-term debt
questions.answer: Current assets minus current liabilities
questions.explanation: Net working capital = Current assets − Current liabilities. It represents the short-term capital available to fund operations after meeting immediate obligations.

questions.question: The cash conversion cycle is calculated as:
options: Days Inventory Outstanding − Days Payable Outstanding
Days Inventory Outstanding + Days Sales Outstanding − Days Payable Outstanding
Days Sales Outstanding + Days Payable Outstanding
Days Payable Outstanding − Days Inventory Outstanding
questions.answer: Days Inventory Outstanding + Days Sales Outstanding − Days Payable Outstanding
questions.explanation: CCC = DIO + DSO − DPO. It measures the net time between paying for inventory and receiving cash from customers — shorter is better for cash flow.

questions.question: A company offering '3/15 net 45' trade terms means:
options: 3% interest on the full amount, paid in 15 installments over 45 months
3% discount if paid in 15 days, otherwise the full amount is due in 45 days
Pay 45% of the invoice in 3 days and 15% in 15 days
The full invoice is due in 45 days with no discount option
questions.answer: 3% discount if paid in 15 days, otherwise the full amount is due in 45 days
questions.explanation: Standard trade credit notation: X/Y net Z means X% discount if paid within Y days; full amount due in Z days. '3/15 net 45' offers a 3% discount for payment within 15 days.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A project with a negative NPV should generally be:" is: Accepted if it has a positive IRR.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Rejected because it destroys shareholder value.

questions.question: The profitability index of a project with NPV = $50,000 and initial investment = $200,000 is:
questions.explanation: PI = (NPV + Initial Investment) / Initial Investment = ($50,000 + $200,000) / $200,000 = $250,000 / $200,000 = 1.25. PI > 1 indicates a value-creating project.

questions.question: According to trade-off theory, the optimal capital structure is achieved when:
options: The marginal tax benefit of additional debt equals the marginal cost of financial distress
The firm eliminates all debt to remove bankruptcy risk
questions.answer: The marginal tax benefit of additional debt equals the marginal cost of financial distress
questions.explanation: Trade-off theory finds the optimal debt level where the last dollar of debt adds exactly as much in tax shield value as it adds in expected distress costs — beyond this point, more debt destroys value.

questions.question: A company with a cash conversion cycle of 90 days should focus on which improvement?
options: Reducing days inventory outstanding and days sales outstanding while extending days payable
questions.answer: Reducing days inventory outstanding and days sales outstanding while extending days payable
questions.explanation: Shortening CCC improves cash flow: sell inventory faster (lower DIO), collect receivables sooner (lower DSO), and take longer to pay suppliers (higher DPO).

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Semi-strong form market efficiency implies that technical analysis (studying price charts) is:" is: Unable to consistently generate above-market returns since prices already reflect all public information.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Unable to consistently generate above-market returns since prices already reflect all public information is correct.

questions.question: In a horizontal merger, antitrust regulators are most concerned about:
options: Reduced competition and increased market power that could harm consumers
The deal's effect on the acquiring firm's dividend policy
How the merger is financed (debt vs. equity)
questions.answer: Reduced competition and increased market power that could harm consumers
questions.explanation: Antitrust review of horizontal mergers (between competitors) focuses on whether the combined firm would have excessive market power to raise prices or reduce quality — harming consumers and competition.

questions.question: A US company expecting to pay €2,000,000 in 90 days can hedge its currency risk by:
options: Buying euros at today's spot rate and holding them
Entering a forward contract to buy €2,000,000 at a locked-in rate in 90 days
Ignoring the exposure since euro fluctuations are usually small
Borrowing dollars and converting them at the future spot rate
questions.answer: Entering a forward contract to buy €2,000,000 at a locked-in rate in 90 days
questions.explanation: A forward contract to buy euros locks in the cost in dollars — eliminating the risk that the euro will appreciate (making the payment more expensive in dollars) before payment is due.

questions.question: A dividend cut is interpreted negatively by financial markets because it:
options: Signals that the company has run out of investment opportunities
Signals that management expects lower future cash flows — an information asymmetry signal
questions.answer: Signals that management expects lower future cash flows — an information asymmetry signal
questions.explanation: Managers have private information about future prospects. Because dividends are costly to maintain, cutting them signals that management expects lower future earnings — markets react negatively.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A firm's Return on Equity (ROE) using the DuPont formula equals:" is: Net income / Sales.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Net profit margin × Asset turnover × Equity multiplier.

questions.question: When comparing bond duration, which bond has HIGHER interest rate sensitivity?
questions.answer: A 10-year bond with a 5% coupon
questions.explanation: Duration (and price sensitivity) increases with longer maturity and lower coupon rates. The 10-year, 5% coupon bond has both the longest term and the lowest coupon — giving it the highest duration.

questions.question: ESG factors are increasingly incorporated into investment analysis because research suggests:
options: ESG always guarantees superior short-term returns
Companies with strong ESG practices may have lower risk profiles and more sustainable long-term performance
ESG reduces a company's revenue by restricting business activities
ESG analysis replaces traditional financial analysis entirely
questions.answer: Companies with strong ESG practices may have lower risk profiles and more sustainable long-term performance
questions.explanation: Growing evidence suggests ESG leaders have lower regulatory risk, better employee retention, stronger brand equity, and more resilient long-term performance — not just ethical advantages.

questions.question: A leveraged buyout (LBO) is an acquisition where:
options: The target company is bought with 100% equity
A significant portion of the purchase price is financed with debt, using target assets as collateral
The acquirer issues new shares to fund the purchase
questions.answer: A significant portion of the purchase price is financed with debt, using target assets as collateral
questions.explanation: In an LBO, private equity firms use large amounts of debt (70–90% of purchase price) to acquire companies, expecting to improve operations and cash flows to service the debt and generate equity returns.

questions.question: The annualized cost of not taking a '1/10 net 60' trade discount is approximately:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 7.4%.

questions.question: The IRR decision rule says to ACCEPT a project when:
options: IRR is less than the WACC
IRR is greater than the WACC (cost of capital)
questions.answer: IRR is greater than the WACC (cost of capital)
questions.explanation: Accept when IRR > WACC: the project earns more than its cost of capital, creating shareholder value. Reject when IRR < WACC — the project destroys value.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a commonly cited motivation for corporate mergers and acquisitions?
options: Reducing the company's employee headcount as the primary goal
questions.answer: Reducing the company's employee headcount as the primary goal
questions.explanation: M&A motivations include synergies, market entry, technology acquisition, and diversification. Layoffs may result from integration, but reducing headcount as the PRIMARY goal is not a legitimate value-creating M&A motive.

questions.question: The interest coverage ratio measures:
options: The firm's ability to pay current liabilities
How many times EBIT covers annual interest expense — a measure of debt service capacity
The ratio of interest income to interest expense
Total debt divided by total equity
questions.answer: How many times EBIT covers annual interest expense — a measure of debt service capacity
questions.explanation: Interest coverage = EBIT / Interest expense. Higher values indicate the firm comfortably covers interest payments; below 1.5x is generally considered financially distressed.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is a characteristic of common stock that preferred stock does NOT have" is: Dividend priority over debt holders.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Voting rights on major corporate decisions.

questions.question: Purchasing Power Parity predicts that over time, a country with higher inflation will have a:
questions.answer: Weaker currency
questions.explanation: PPP predicts that higher domestic inflation erodes the currency's purchasing power — over time, the exchange rate should depreciate to keep the real price of goods equal across countries.

questions.question: A company has a P/E of 25 while its industry average P/E is 15. This suggests the market believes the company:
options: Is overvalued and should be avoided
Has lower growth prospects than peers
Has higher expected growth or quality justifying a premium
questions.answer: Has higher expected growth or quality justifying a premium
questions.explanation: A higher-than-average P/E implies investors are willing to pay more per dollar of earnings — typically because they expect higher future earnings growth or lower risk than comparable companies.

questions.question: An ethical financial manager, when facing a decision that maximizes short-term profits but harms employees, should:
options: Always choose maximum profit as the sole criterion
Consider all stakeholders' interests and assess the long-term impact on firm reputation and sustainability
Do whatever is legally required, nothing more
Let shareholders vote on every ethical decision
questions.answer: Consider all stakeholders' interests and assess the long-term impact on firm reputation and sustainability
questions.explanation: Ethical financial management balances stakeholder interests — short-term profit maximization at the expense of employees or communities often creates long-term risks (reputational, regulatory, talent) that reduce overall firm value.
